title: Exercises: One-Time Signatures

exercises.title: Complete Lamport Implementation

testCases.input: l = Lamport(256); sk, pk = l.keygen(); len(sk) == 256
l = Lamport(256); sk, pk = l.keygen(); sig = l.sign(sk, b'test'); l.verify(pk, b'test', sig)

hints: KeyGen: Generate 2n random values, hash each for pk
Sign: For each bit i of H(msg), reveal sk[i][bit]
Verify: Check H(sig[i]) == pk[i][bit] for all i

exercises.title: WOTS Implementation
exercises.description: Implement Winternitz OTS with configurable parameter w.
exercises.task: Create a complete WOTS implementation with checksum.
exercises.starterCode:
import hashlib
import secrets
import math
from typing import Tuple, List

class WOTS:
    def __init__(self, n: int = 256, w: int = 16):
        self.n = n
        self.w = w
        self.hash_bytes = n // 8
        self.log_w = int(math.log2(w))
        self.l1 = math.ceil(n / self.log_w)
        self.l2 = math.floor(math.log2(self.l1 * (w-1)) / self.log_w) + 1
        self.l = self.l1 + self.l2
    
    def hash(self, data: bytes) -> bytes:
        return hashlib.sha256(data).digest()[:self.hash_bytes]
    
    def chain(self, x: bytes, steps: int) -> bytes:
        """Apply hash 'steps' times."""
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def to_base_w(self, data: bytes, length: int) -> List[int]:
        """Convert bytes to base-w digits."""
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def keygen(self) -> Tuple[List[bytes], List[bytes]]:
        """Generate (sk, pk)."""
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def sign(self, sk: List[bytes], msg: bytes) -> List[bytes]:
        """Sign with checksum."""
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def verify(self, pk: List[bytes], msg: bytes, sig: List[bytes]) -> bool:
        """Verify signature."""

exercises.solution:
import hashlib
import secrets
import math
from typing import Tuple, List

class WOTS:
    def __init__(self, n: int = 256, w: int = 16):
        self.n = n
        self.w = w
        self.hash_bytes = n // 8
        self.log_w = int(math.log2(w))
        self.l1 = math.ceil(n / self.log_w)
        self.l2 = math.floor(math.log2(self.l1 * (w-1)) / self.log_w) + 1
        self.l = self.l1 + self.l2
    
    def hash(self, data: bytes) -> bytes:
        return hashlib.sha256(data).digest()[:self.hash_bytes]
    
    def chain(self, x: bytes, steps: int) -> bytes:
        result = x
        for _ in range(steps):
            result = self.hash(result)
        return result
    
    def to_base_w(self, data: bytes, length: int) -> List[int]:
        num = int.from_bytes(data, 'big')
        digits = []
        for _ in range(length):
            digits.append(num % self.w)
            num //= self.w
        return digits[::-1]
    
    def keygen(self) -> Tuple[List[bytes], List[bytes]]:
        sk = [secrets.token_bytes(self.hash_bytes) for _ in range(self.l)]
        pk = [self.chain(sk[i], self.w - 1) for i in range(self.l)]
        return sk, pk
    
    def sign(self, sk: List[bytes], msg: bytes) -> List[bytes]:
        h = self.hash(msg)
        msg_digits = self.to_base_w(h, self.l1)
        
        checksum = sum(self.w - 1 - d for d in msg_digits)
        cs_bytes = checksum.to_bytes(4, 'big')
        cs_digits = self.to_base_w(cs_bytes, self.l2)
        
        all_digits = msg_digits + cs_digits
        return [self.chain(sk[i], all_digits[i]) for i in range(self.l)]
    
    def verify(self, pk: List[bytes], msg: bytes, sig: List[bytes]) -> bool:
        h = self.hash(msg)
        msg_digits = self.to_base_w(h, self.l1)
        
        checksum = sum(self.w - 1 - d for d in msg_digits)
        cs_bytes = checksum.to_bytes(4, 'big')
        cs_digits = self.to_base_w(cs_bytes, self.l2)
        
        all_digits = msg_digits + cs_digits
        
        for i in range(self.l):
            remaining = self.w - 1 - all_digits[i]
            if self.chain(sig[i], remaining) != pk[i]:
                return False
        return True

testCases.input: w = WOTS(256, 16); sk, pk = w.keygen(); sig = w.sign(sk, b'test'); w.verify(pk, b'test', sig)

hints: Checksum: sum(w-1 - digit) for all message digits
Verify: complete chain from sig to end, compare to pk

exercises.title: OTS Security Demonstrator
exercises.description: Demonstrate why OTS is one-time and checksum prevents forgery.
exercises.task: Implement demonstrations of OTS vulnerabilities when misused.
exercises.starterCode:
import hashlib
import secrets
from typing import List, Set, Tuple

class OTSSecurityDemo:
    def __init__(self):
        pass
    
    def demonstrate_reuse_vulnerability(self, num_signatures: int = 2) -> dict:
        """
        Show how signing multiple messages with same Lamport key
        leaks the entire secret key.
        
        Returns statistics about exposed secrets.
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
    
    def demonstrate_wots_no_checksum_attack(self) -> bool:
        """
        Show that WOTS without checksum allows forgery.
        
        Returns True if forgery succeeded.
        """
        # Your code here
        pass
exercises.solution:
import hashlib
import secrets
from typing import List, Set, Tuple

class OTSSecurityDemo:
    def __init__(self, n: int = 32):  # Small n for demo
        self.n = n
        self.hash_bytes = 4
    
    def hash(self, data: bytes) -> bytes:
        return hashlib.sha256(data).digest()[:self.hash_bytes]
    
    def demonstrate_reuse_vulnerability(self, num_signatures: int = 2) -> dict:
        # Generate Lamport keys
        sk = [(secrets.token_bytes(self.hash_bytes),
               secrets.token_bytes(self.hash_bytes)) for _ in range(self.n)]
        pk = [(self.hash(sk[i][0]), self.hash(sk[i][1])) for i in range(self.n)]
        
        exposed_0 = set()
        exposed_1 = set()
        
        for sig_num in range(num_signatures):
            msg = secrets.token_bytes(16)
            h = self.hash(msg)
            
            for i in range(self.n):
                bit = (h[i // 8] >> (7 - i % 8)) & 1
                if bit == 0:
                    exposed_0.add(i)
                else:
                    exposed_1.add(i)
        
        both_exposed = exposed_0 & exposed_1
        
        return {
            'signatures': num_signatures,
            'positions_with_sk0_exposed': len(exposed_0),
            'positions_with_sk1_exposed': len(exposed_1),
            'positions_fully_exposed': len(both_exposed),
            'can_forge_any_message': len(both_exposed) == self.n
        }
    
    def demonstrate_wots_no_checksum_attack(self) -> bool:
        w = 16
        # Simplified WOTS without checksum
        sk = [secrets.token_bytes(self.hash_bytes) for _ in range(8)]
        pk = [sk[i] for i in range(8)]
        for i in range(8):
            for _ in range(w - 1):
                pk[i] = self.hash(pk[i])
        
        # Sign a message
        msg1 = b'original'
        h1 = self.hash(msg1)
        digits1 = [(h1[i // 2] >> (4 * (1 - i % 2))) & 0xF for i in range(8)]
        
        sig1 = [sk[i] for i in range(8)]
        for i in range(8):
            for _ in range(digits1[i]):
                sig1[i] = self.hash(sig1[i])
        
        # Attack: forge signature on message with higher digits
        msg2 = b'forgery!'
        h2 = self.hash(msg2)
        digits2 = [(h2[i // 2] >> (4 * (1 - i % 2))) & 0xF for i in range(8)]
        
        sig2 = []
        for i in range(8):
            if digits2[i] >= digits1[i]:
                # Can forge by hashing forward
                val = sig1[i]
                for _ in range(digits2[i] - digits1[i]):
                    val = self.hash(val)
                sig2.append(val)
            else:
                # Would need to go backwards - can't forge
                return False
        
        return True
hints: Track which secret values are exposed across signatures
After enough signatures, all positions have both values exposed
Without checksum, increasing digits is free (just hash more)
